sentences.text: Yes, we tried to volunteer to go for registration-- and myself, one of my uncles, Uncle Sam, Samuel, and my brother-in-law, Adolf.
Altria was the wife, and at that time there was one child.
And my future wife stayed on the collective farm and doing the things, working or whatever.
So there, there was no transportation, really.
So we really had to improvise ourselves.
And it was impossible to walk there during the night, I mean, during the day, strictly walking during the night.
And the railroad was there.
There was a railroad tracks there.
There were railroad tracks, but unfortunately, there was no regular railroad going.
So we improvise.
We walk.
Most of the time, we walk during the night on the railroad tracks because otherwise, you wouldn't be able to find.
On the railroad tracks and to the vicinity.
It was not too far away.
It was 35 kilometers, which is not a big distance.
And I think it took something like two nights to get there.
So eventually we got there.
And of course, we were registered.
And as far as I know, we were accepted because they cut our hair.
So we were accepted.
We passed the medical exams.
And we were asked politely to wait our turn.
Go back home.
They have the addresses.
There was no telephone lines.
There were just addresses.
And we would be notified by courier or by a letter, whichever.
And that was the end of it.
So we picked ourselves, hoping that we're going to, you know, be ready to go in a day or two.
They knew how many people we were there in the farm.

So the whole family-- what good was with this army is, if the army goes, wherever it goes, at that time everybody knew that they are going to go to Iran and to Palestine because at that particular time, they supposed to have fought before that on Eastern Front but unfortunately, didn't come out because they didn't have enough soldiers.
So they decided to wait and go when they, when they have more divisions than just one.
In any event, we didn't know it then.
This is after the facts.
I'm talking here about the facts.
We didn't know anything about this secret, what was going on, and everything else.
In any event, the army was there.
It was organized, and it was moved-- the first segments of it, like 40,000, whatever it is, was moved to Iran.
From Iran it was moved to Palestine.
And families did follow.
And unfortunately, we did not get notification.
Another transport went out, altogether, as we found out later, something like 70,000 soldiers were moved to Iran, Palestine, and then whatever, to Britain and wherever they were sent.
And unfortunately, and the families.
All total as I found out later was something like 140,000 people, with the families.
And among the people was also, as you probably know, was Menachem Begin, one of our future, past, Prime Ministers of Israel.
He was, as a matter of fact, in a camp about 80 kilometers away from us.
So he was there.
There were a couple other people which was together with us on the camp.
He was there.
They, they moved.
They moved.
My professor, which was Professor Landfish, he was able to move also with the army.
And we found out that there was a quota on the Jewish people.
Simply as that.
There was simply quota.

And so many people were allowed, and so many people were disallowed because I had friends which served in the Polish army in 1939, and they had ranks of corporal and everything else, friends of ours, which they were also rejected.
Not rejected.
Nobody was rejected unless he didn't pass the medical exam.
They were just simply put on the waiting line.
Was part of the registration, they ask what your religion was?
Oh, yes.
So they knew who was Jewish.
Oh, yes, absolutely.
That's, that's important.
The first thing which came, your religion.
That's-- was mosaic.
At that time it was said, mosaic religion.
That's, that's it.
That was, that was part of the whole thing.
We were, yeah, we were left on the, on the [?
Chem ?] farm, and we found out that the end of the army is over.
There was no future for us.
There was no food for us, too, on the farm itself.
We just struggled.
We made it.
And thanks to the thing that I knew tailoring and one of my uncles knew tailoring.
We were able to improvise and get some food in because we did things for the manager of the farm and, of course, for the people which are in the top, in the, in the, in the government there.
Plain people had no, no, no chance.
So we were able to get some food for that.
No money, just food.
Everything was for food.
And saw there's no future there.
One of my uncles-- I've mentioned too many uncles, but there are only two which were involved in the whole thing-- Uncle Sam, which was Samuel, and David.
Uncle David decided, because they had a large family, they decided to stay put in the capital city in that region where we were in Siberia.
And they stayed put.
Now, we had to go back because we were in communication with them, and we know that, we knew that they're doing OK.
There's enough food there.
They are not starving.
And there are jobs.
And there are jobs.
So we send letters to him.
And you need a special pass already.
Going back is already not free.
You have to have a special pass from the [INAUDIBLE] And they have to have a special letter written by the, by the, whoever, from the employment office, that, yes indeed, there is a job for them.
And then you have to have a special letter, there is quarters for you to live in, that you don't have to rely on looking for quarters.
You have to have provision that you have quarters.
So that uncle was able to provide us with all those things, documents.
There had to be a round stamp and everything else.
And unfortunately, you know, unfortunately, you have to, you have to, you have to cheat a little bit when you're, when you are in a, in a jam.
So he sent-- they sent us documents with a square stamp.
A square stamp was no good.
So square stamp was no good.
But my-- well, my brother and I was very good at this thing.
Yeah, he just simply-- Uzbeks didn't read Russian.
So that was OK.
Only for the Russian guys, which they-- for the, I mean, for the managers there that they should give us a release.
We need that thing, yeah.
So he grabbed one of those manager's stamp.
He didn't watch it.
It was a round stamp.
He just put it on top of the other one, just barely.
As long as there was a ring was fine.
And that's how we made it.
We made it back to, to Ioshkar-Ola.
That was the main city.
And we got established.
Myself, I got a job.
And my uncle also got a job in the same factory, as a matter of fact.
And later, my wife taught, was taught how to sew, and she was doing that too.
And at that time already my other sister-in-law was already pregnant.
So she had a baby.
So she didn't work.
Baby-- people with babies didn't work.
They were staying home.
And my other brother-in-law, because he was not a tailor, so he had a job in, again, with horses.
They needed horses there, a lot of horses so they needed people to work with because all the men were on the front lines.
You didn't have any Russian men in that town.
They were all on the front lines.
In any event, every got, everybody got the jobs.
And we were doing fine.
We got paid and everything.
And the war was progressing.
And I was getting impatient, you know, being young and over myself, I was getting impatient.
I got a note, first of all, from the embassy, from the Polish Embassy in Kuibyshev that they're-- another army-- they're going to, they're going to call another army.
And that second one will be organized.
And voluntarily, of course.
Voluntarily.
The First Division was voluntarily.
Then the First Division is going to be organized.
And I got that letter.
And I send away that I'm willing to volunteer to go to the army.
I send it away, and I was called.
That was in November-- November or December.
I was called in in March of 1943.
And that was my beginning of my army duty.
The rest of them stayed behind in that town.
I went all by myself.
Nobody was happy.
And I wasn't happy either.
But, to me, my first thing is to go and, and get my people.
Go to my home.
Go to my people.
That was my only aim.
I was too young.
I was very stupid and maybe, too, to go volunteer.
And this time of the time, of the year, because it was still very deep.
Stalingrad was still in German hands.
Yes, then already we knew.
Well, not in the camps as far as the camps, we're talking?
Or the front lines?
Front lines, yes.
Front lines we knew because there were good things and bad things.
The good things we knew, and we knew how far the Germans were.
We knew how far the Germans were.
But there were good things, of course, that were immediately announced.
But the rest was written in the, in the newspapers, which I was able to read.
And the same thing was also broadcast through the radio in the shop.
We had a radio.
We didn't have a radio at home, but in the shop there was a radio.
And of course, they were broadcasting good things.
And they were saying where the front line is.
No, no.
We didn't know anything until we entered the camp, which was in 1944.
We had absolutely no knowledge.
No, no word on it.
The name of the division was the First Division by the name of Tadeusz Kosciuszko.
Tadeusz Kosciuszko.
It was named after a big Polish hero which really fought here in the Revolutionary War in the United States.
And the other army, which was organized, which I first registered, was Sokorski.
He was the general, and he was the head of the government of the Polish government in London.
Now, it was organized by people, by one of the-- at that time, he was a colonel, and his name was Zygmunt Berling.
Berling was his name.
He was the Chief of Staff of the General Anders, which he was in charge of the Polish army which went out.
He was his chief of staff.
And he decided to stay behind.
He said, the closest way to Poland is not through the Western front but through the Eastern front.
The way he saw it.
He was not a Communist or any political leader.
He was just brave soldier.

As a matter of fact, both of those gentlemen which would come out later in the story, both of them, Anders and Berling, with 300, 398 other people, high offices, were in the Katyn forest, if you're familiar with Katyn forest.
That will come out later.
Where both of them in the same boat, and they were taking out-- those 400 officers were taking out by the Russians before anything started.
Even with the First-- with the First Army, they had something in plan so they took out 400 of those people.
And the rest, we know there what happened to them.
So they both were in Katyn forest.
Survived.
He was a colonel, a professional soldier during the, you know, in the First World War.
I mean, in the First World War and in the second.
In the first World War, he was with Pilsudski.
One promise.
That this group will not go to the West.
This group has to-- it has to fight on the Eastern Front because they needed people.
They lost awful lot of people, and they needed people.
He says, on the one condition, only if this army goes directly on the Eastern Front.
And that's how they organized them.
So in any event, I entered the First Division, First Infantry Regiment.
And I was assigned as a gunner to a rocket launcher.
I was with that unit for six weeks.
Then, for some reason, I was chosen.
I was chosen to enter the non-commissioned academy, Ander camp.
Non-commissioned academy.
There were about approximately, I don't know, about 800 other ones which entered that-- which was designated to that academy.
They didn't ask me any questions.
I don't know if I was worthy of it or not.
But anyway, they saw something which I didn't see.
I was there.
You don't say no in the army.
So I was entered as a gunner in that particular unit, still with the rocket launches.
And we were at the, in the academy from, approximately from June, from June till, till September, September 12 or something like that.
Then the whole unit, the whole academy were destined for the front lines.
We got our order approximately the 12th of September.
Maybe it's not accurate date, but I know it was beginning of September.
To the front line as a unit.
And we entered the front line near Smolensk, in the forest of Smolensk.
And our first front line assignment was October 12, 1942.
I mean 1943.
I am so sorry. 1943.
October 12, 1943, that was our first assignment, front line assignment.
And to say that anybody was not scared would be a big lie.
Everybody was scared.
But nevertheless, if we didn't think about it, we all thought that whoever it goes comes back.
Unfortunately, it was a very, very tough assignment.
We were able-- now, we were in the center.
The two other divisions, Russian divisions was one on the left flank, one on the right flank.
We were in the center, and we had to take our line, period.
They were responsible for their position.
We were responsible for our position.
We were able to advance on that particular line five kilometers, which was not a lot, you know, to our advantage.
But nevertheless, it was a breakthrough.
And the Russians could do it.
They advanced couple of hundred yards, maybe a mile, maybe one kilometer.
And they couldn't do it any farther.
We were able to advance.
Unfortunately, we lost 35% of our people.
We lost 40.
I was in my unit, which is a small unit really.
We lost 40 people, dead, plus about 35 was wounded.
Nevertheless, we were able to move.
We never relinquished the position to the Germans.
The Russians took our positions.
They held them.
That front was a very vicious front line because we could not get any tank support.
It was marsh land there-- the marshes of Pripjet, which was one of the worst marshes in Europe, really-- and a tank simply couldn't make it.
Small artillery like the 76-millimeter gun was able.
A heavy howitzer couldn't do it either.
It just sunk.
So, at any rate, we stayed there for a day and a half.
After a day and a half, we were replaced by the Russians.
And we moved to the back, about 20 kilometers back, to recover wounds and to be replenished with manpower and munitions.
And by the way, this is that name which I have here.
I have to look at that name to give you the name of the rabbi.
We had only one rabbi there, the unit.
Unfortunately, this rabbi did not make it.
He was a young rabbi, and his name was Hirsz Zawada.
And he fell in the battle for Lenin, October 12, 1943, including, including 56 of other Jewish soldiers.
I'm going to tell you the truth.
Absolutely none whatsoever.
Honest to God.
There was no discrimination there.
The reason for it, I don't know what's the reason for it.
First, we were in the same shoe, in the same boat, let's put it this way.
All of us-- now, in my unit, I was the youngest in my unit, and I had a very responsible position because I was the gunner in that unit, which, you know, I'm talking about [? buddy ?] unit there.
But in any event, there was no discrimination for one reason.
I guess all of us were there in Siberia.
All of us suffered the same hunger and the same sorrows.
So I guess that's the only reason which, which there was no discrimination.
There was no discrimination.
If there was, maybe it was hidden.
Maybe it was not shown.
I don't know.
I personally did not came across.
Some-- nobody called me a dirty Jew or whatever it is.
No, nobody did.
Very strange because I was lecturing in one of the-- I'm thinking in Kingston.
I was invited the last year.
And I had-- you know, there was a question and answer there at the end of the lecture.
And one of the gentlemen is there.
I have it, as a matter of fact, on the tape.
One of the gentlemen there asked me the same thing.
He says, I was in the United States Army, and to tell you the truth, I felt it in the United States Army during the war.
So I said to him the same thing.
Maybe, maybe they was.
But it was not shown.
Maybe it was not shown because first of all, we were both soldiers on the front lines.
And both of us went through hell.
And both of us know what we are fighting here for.
And both of them didn't know if they're going to live next day.
So it was not shown.
If he was on the front line or not, this I don't know.
But in fact, that was the fact-- that I did not see any anti-Semitism was there.
Now, who, what was the name of the officer in charge of your unit that you moved with?
Who gave the orders for your group?
For my group, [INAUDIBLE] was the general, he was the colonel, Zygmunt Berling.
He was the colonel.
He was taking orders.
He definitely, he was taking orders.
He couldn't do anything on his own.
He got his assignment, and his assignment was take it.
Don't leave it.
Just take it.
If you will need help, we will give you help.
But this is your assignment.
You are responsible for this assignment.
General Rokossovsky.
Marshal Rokossovsky later.
Rokossovsky was his-- that was his immediate superior.
After I went-- after I, we recovered, there were a lot of-- in between, there were a lot of people which were missing in certain units.
And I knew that they were looking for people in artillery.
Also, as a [INAUDIBLE] in any kind because they lost people.
So I registered to the-- from the academy, I registered to enter a artillery regiment, artillery, artillery battery.
And so I-- you have to go to the staff, and you have to register, put your name on it.
And they saw that I am from the school.
So he says, I don't know if I'll be able to transfer you because the examinations.
In another couple of weeks, you'll be finished with the examination.
I had already three stars, three stripes, but I was looking for the fourth one.
And I didn't care for the fourth one.
I just wanted to get out.
I said, too many of my colleagues are being killed in the infantry.
So I registered, and there was a nice young lady there, which, by the way, was, was also Jewish.
And she said, I don't know if I can get through but just strictly because you are from the school.
But I will put your name.
If you're called through, that's fine with me.
And sure enough, I went through.
And about three weeks later, I was assigned to the First Artillery Regiment, Battery Number Two, as a gunner.
It was a howitzer cannon.
And it was a three-ton howitzer cannon which was driven by American Buicks and Studebakers.
And we had 88 men.
We had nine Jewish soldiers there in between.
I was the only one which manned a cannon.
And I was the youngest in that unit.
We had people there which were veterans from World War II, you know, from the Polish army, soldiers.
But nevertheless, I had the assignment, and I had to sign a special paper that I'm responsible for this gun.
Under no condition that gun will fall into enemy hands.
And we had two shells, special shells for destruction.
And, thank God, we didn't have to use it.
There were a couple of times that we came pretty close because we were surrounded and a couple of times we had to leave in a hurry.
But thank God, we didn't have to use it, and I didn't use it.
So that, those guns-- those shells were surrounded, surrendered after the war.
So that was my responsibility.
And we had 88 people, a self, self-sufficient unit with our own kitchen and everything.
Of course, we didn't have any homes to live in.
But we had that.
And, thank God, with this unit, with this unit actually I went through the whole war.

And we were starting, starting in the Smolensk area, occupying, you know, the, the west, the east, eastern, not the-- well, the west part of Poland, like cities Lublin, Chelm, Wlodawa, and Warsaw, and going all the way down from that area to [NON-ENGLISH] and so forth, the port city of Szczecin, and all the way down to Berlin.
And finally, we terminated our stint in Berlin with this particular gun.
Yes, absolutely.
There were great fighting in Russia and smaller advancements, not bigger advancement.
The advancement came mostly in spring because that was wintertime.
And in wintertime, you couldn't do too much because there was just too much snow.
In wintertime, we had to stay 24 hours guard and just shoveling the snow down with wooden shovels because there's a tremendous amount of snow in that particular time, you know, in 1943, '44.
Spring '44.
Absolutely.
Spring, '44.
And finally, we came to the, to the borders.
We actually came to the Polish borders in July of 1944.
In July of 1944.
July of 1944.
We crossed the border in, by Bug, the river Bug.
And the immediate city, the first city, the first city was Chelm, from this side.
Then Wlodawa.
And then, somehow we really didn't know anything about concentration camp or any other thing because we saw hundreds and hundreds of mass graves in the Soviet Union, in White Russia.
But we never saw anything, never heard anything about concentration camp.
Yes, we saw the graves.
We saw the graves.
Yes, they knew about it.
And they told us about what had happened.
We didn't know how many Jews are in that particular grave because they were killing the Russians and they were killing the association.
In other words, if somebody was associated, a Communist or something like, he got shot.
And of course, they liquidated all the Jews out of Majdanek.
There's no question about it.
All the Jews out of Majdanek, they were liquidated, plus there were some Russians.
And not only that, we saw people hanging still on the trees, which they cut.
But then we were told those are partisans, the underground workers.
Hundreds of thousands of those, of those ravines.
Some of them were open.
Some of them were open.
And some of them were just slightly covered.
And the snow melted, and you could see what's going on there.
It was sticking out.
That was the first site.
Mass murder, we found out in the Soviet Union and Jews and on the population.
But the Jews were liquidated automatically.
In Russia, it was very easy.
Russia had the same system.
You could not call a Jew 'Jew.' But on your papers, on your papers, your religion was mentioned.
In other words, if you were Jewish, you said you're [NON-ENGLISH] and if you were Uzbek, you said 'Uzbek.' It was on your documents.
There was no cheating there.
Some of the Russian Jews, you know, down to the commoners, they didn't have the circumcision, so they could not be recognized.
But in any event, they had it in the document that they were Jews.
Absolutely.
There were still Russian people alive.
You didn't see any men.
You saw men are only with guns, partisans.
You only saw women or children.
I did not see men free except when the guy was 75 or 80 years old, you saw other men.
But that's about all we saw of men.
But people were there.
People were there.
We stayed-- there was a time that we stayed in-- near [NON-ENGLISH].
The front advanced towards, towards our line to the Polish border.
And when there was a long stay, we stayed in the farmer's place.
You know, like, we got in like 10 soldiers, 12 soldiers, 15 soldiers, whatever it is, we stayed in the farmer's place overnight.
During the day, we moved out from there, went to the guns.
The following day, the same thing.
They would tell us everything.
They would tell us the stories.
They would tell us what was going on there.
And you could see because there was a lot of destruction-- houses burnt, people hanging, so you really could, visually you could see.
And all those graves.
Thank you.
